title: Pro-Life Idaho Rep. Brent Crane Insults and Intimidates Family, Promises to Keep Abortion Legal
author: James Silberman
orientation: An Idaho meeting between Rep. Brent Crane and the Herndon family ended with Crane insulting the family, calling state patrol on them and their 10-year-old daughter, and vowing the Abortion Human Rights Act would never get a hearing while he chairs the committee. The article uses the incident to argue that hostility to abolition is common even among pro-life conservative Republicans.
audiences: skeptics new to the abolitionist movement

questions.question: What were the Herndons asking Idaho legislators to do at the Capitol that day?
questions.answer: They were lobbying for the Abortion Human Rights Act, which would abolish abortion outright, while urging legislators to reject an incremental pro-life bill they believed would do more harm than good.
questions.quote: The Herndons were asking officials to support the bill to abolish abortion (the Abortion Human Rights Act) in Idaho and not to support an incremental pro-life bill which would do more harm than good.

questions.question: What position did Crane take on whether mothers should face criminal liability for abortion?
questions.answer: Crane held the standard pro-life position that only abortion doctors should be punished, leaving mothers free from any legal repercussions for procuring the killing of their children.
questions.quote: Crane, like many pro-life politicians, believes that mothers should be allowed to murder children with impunity and that it should only be illegal for doctors to murder children.

questions.question: How did Crane respond when the Herndons pressed the abolitionist case for equal protection?
questions.answer: He shifted from policy disagreement into personal attack, accusing Scott and Arlene of pride and arrogance before escalating further by calling security without warning.
questions.quote: At this point, Crane began to “devolve in his attitude,” calling Scott and Arlene prideful and arrogant. A few minutes later, without any warning, Crane called security on Scott, Arlene, and Esther.

questions.question: Why did Arlene leave the office before officers arrived?
questions.answer: She wanted to defuse the situation and protect her 10-year-old daughter Esther from the spectacle of armed officers being summoned over a meeting Crane himself had invited them to.
questions.quote: I have my daughter here. We don’t need to scare her with men with weapons and badges and things.

questions.question: What threat did Crane make against the Abortion Human Rights Act?
questions.answer: He explicitly told Scott Herndon that, as committee chairman, he would refuse to let the bill receive a hearing as long as abolitionists continued opposing pro-life legislation.
questions.quote: I’m livid, and as long as I’m chairman of this committee, your bill will never be heard. And if you go about what you’re doing [opposing pro-life bills], I will never hear your bill. Period.

questions.question: Why does the article argue that calling security was a tactic of intimidation rather than a genuine safety concern?
questions.audience: skeptics new to the abolitionist movement
questions.answer: Because Crane kept actively engaging Scott in conversation even after summoning officers, the author concludes the call was meant to physically intimidate rather than remove a real threat.
questions.quote: Crane calling security while continuing to speak with Scott shows that he knows there was no actual need for security. He simply wanted men with guns present to physically intimidate the Herndon family.

questions.question: What two motives does the article suggest could explain Crane's behavior?
questions.answer: Either conviction over his own complicity in failing to abolish abortion, or anger that abolitionists have undermined the political theater that lets pro-life politicians be celebrated for ineffective laws.
questions.quote: 1. Deeply convicted about his own sin. He knows he should support the abolition of abortion in Idaho but doesn’t have the courage to do so, or 2. Angry that the Herndons and other Idaho abolitionists have [ruined their game](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HQeJ_xrYvW4).

questions.question: How does the article characterize Crane's broader political profile to make its point that hostility to abolition isn't a fringe RINO trait?
questions.audience: skeptics new to the abolitionist movement
questions.answer: It catalogs his strong conservative credentials—pro-life bill author, March for Life attendee, NRA-rated, ACU-rated, Trump supporter—to argue he is a textbook conservative Republican, not an outlier.
questions.quote: He received a 100 percent rating with the NRA and an [88 percent rating](https://freethestates.org/acuratings.conservative.org/wp-content/uploads/sites/5/2020/12/CLA_Idaho_2020_web-1.pdf) from the American Conservative Union in 2020. He is a huge Trump supporter and believes the election was a [fraud](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rB3F2nxhDCk). He [opposes](https://www.idahopress.com/news/local/idaho-house-votes-to-remove-all-covid-19-gathering-limits-statewide/article_ae99a0fb-dc80-5571-9fef-a579a192c754.html) the Covid lockdowns. He is no “RINO.” He is a standard conservative Republican legislator.

questions.question: What broader pattern about pro-life politicians does the article want readers to take away from this incident?
questions.answer: That open desire to keep abortion legal coupled with hatred of abolitionists is widespread even among self-identified pro-life and very conservative officials.
questions.quote: That may be a shock for some who are new to the Abolitionist Movement, but this desire to keep abortion legal and intense hatred for abolitionists is not uncommon among pro-life politicians and lobbyists, even very pro-life and very conservative ones.

questions.question: What concrete actions does the article ask Idaho readers to take?
questions.answer: Sign the petition supporting Idaho's abolition bill through Abolish Abortion Idaho, the organization run by the Herndons, and spread the article to others who live in the state.
questions.quote: If you live in Idaho, make sure to [sign the petition to support Idaho’s abolition bill and receive updates from Abolish Abortion Idaho](https://www.abolishabortionid.com/petition-contact/), an organization run by the Herndons. If you don’t live in Idaho, send this article to someone who does.

related.title: Idaho Pro-Life State Senator Says Ending Abortion “Destroys the Pro-Life Movement”
related.reason: Another Idaho pro-life legislator caught openly opposing the Abortion Human Rights Act, reinforcing the article's claim that Crane's hostility is a pattern, not an exception.

related.title: Pro-Life Idaho State Rep: "We all know nullification doesn't happen in reality."
related.reason: Direct companion piece on Idaho pro-life legislators rejecting the same abolition strategy the Herndons brought to Crane.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Explains the abolitionist objection to the incremental pro-life bill the Herndons were specifically asking Crane to reject.

related.title: Equal Protection
related.reason: The article links to the abolitionist criminalization position; this is the canonical explainer of why mothers must not be exempted from prosecution.

related.title: Utah/Idaho Baptist Convention Calls UT-ID Governors, Legislature to Abolish Abortion
related.reason: Later Idaho coverage involving Scott Herndon and the abolition push, showing the movement's continued pressure on the legislature Crane chairs.

related.title: June V. Russo Must Be the Death Knell of Pro-Life Judicial Supremacy and Incrementalism
related.reason: Broader strategic critique of the incrementalist pro-life politics Crane represents.

related.title: Watch: Incremental Pro-Life Strategy Failure
related.reason: Documents another concrete failure of the incrementalist approach the Herndons were warning Crane against.
